text: All throughout the world, from the UK to
Australia to Canada to here in the US,
we've been seeing various corporations
and governments trying to roll out new
measures to spy on people to collect
their biometric data and even enact
censorship all under the gist their lie
of wanting to protect kids. Like I said,
we all know that's a lot of hot air. And
now we have the California government
escalating things even further as we
have a new California law saying that
all operating systems including Linux
need to have some form of age
verification at account setup. This all
coming from Assembly Bill number 1043
chapter 675. Put simply once again they
want this age verification at account
setup via the OS where the OS would then
send a secure real-time digital quote
unquote signal to app developers when
requested. Developers then treat that
signal as the main age indicator to
comply with further child privacy laws
like COPA and California's age
appropriate design code. And yes, this
is already signed into law, effective
fully next year in 2027, having passed
with full bipartisan support. Not a
single politician voted against the
bill. Now, there's been a number of
journalists covering this topic, saying
things like, "Hey, at least California
isn't asking for your driver's license
or your face scan or anything like
that." My response is, first of all,
this is already overstepping at a
baseline by default. But I could take it
even further because you notice how
these corporations and governments, they
never ask for less information. It's
always more. What I'm clearly getting at
is in a few years, California could also
turn around and escalate this even more,
saying something like, "Hey, our checks
weren't robust enough. Now, yeah, we do
require your ID or your face scan or
your bank statement, whatever it may
be." And there's more that I could be
saying about all of this. However,
trying to keep the video as brief and to
the point as possible, and I do think
that's all the most important stuff. So,
I'll wrap this one up. Let me know what
you think. Thank you for tuning in and
I'll see you in the next
